title: How to Use Active Recall for Free Response Questions
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You aced the multiple-choice section. Then you got to the short answer questions and froze. Your mind went blank. You knew the material, but you couldn't pull it out of your brain on command. Multiple-choice gives you clues. Free response leaves you stranded. Here's how to practice for the harder format.

The Problem with Multiple-Choice Studying

Multiple-choice tests recognition. You see the right answer among four options. Your brain just has to recognize it, not produce it. That's why you can do well on multiple-choice even if you didn't study that hard.

Free response tests recall. You have to produce the answer from nothing. No clues. No hints. Just your memory. This is much harder — and it's what you need to practice.

If you only study with flashcards or multiple-choice practice, you're training recognition, not recall. On test day, you'll struggle.

The Blank Page Method

After studying a chapter, close the book. Take out a blank sheet of paper. Write down everything you remember. No prompts. No hints. Just whatever comes out of your brain.

This is called 'free recall.' It's uncomfortable. Your brain will resist. That's the point. The struggle is what builds strong memories.

After you've written everything you can, open the book. Check what you missed. Add it to your notes in a different color. Those gaps are what you need to study more.

Do this for each chapter. By test day, you'll be able to produce the information on command.

Turn Your Notes into Questions

Instead of reviewing your notes as statements, turn them into questions. Write the question on one side of a flashcard (or in a document), and the answer on the other side.

For example, instead of 'The mitochondria is the powerhouse of the cell,' write 'What is the function of the mitochondria?' Now you're practicing recall, not recognition.

Go through your questions. Answer out loud or in writing. Check your answer. If you got it wrong, put the card back in the deck. Repeat until you get it right.

Predict Free Response Questions

Teachers are predictable. They have favorite questions. Before the test, look at your study guide or chapter objectives. Turn each objective into a potential free response question.

If the objective says 'Explain the causes of World War I,' that's almost certainly going to be a short answer or essay question. Practice answering it. Write a full paragraph. Time yourself.

Do this for every major topic. You'll walk into the test having already written the answers.

The 'Blurting' Method for Essays

For essay-style free response, use blurting. Set a timer for 10 minutes. Write everything you know about the topic without stopping. Don't worry about organization or complete sentences. Just get the information out.

Then organize your blurt into an outline. Then write the essay. This process forces you to recall information before you have to structure it — which is exactly what happens on test day.

Practice with Past Tests

Ask your teacher for old tests. Many teachers reuse question formats. If not, look online for sample free response questions in your subject. AP Central has decades of past free response questions for AP classes.

Take the free response section under timed conditions. No notes. No pauses. Then grade yourself honestly. Review what you missed. Repeat.

The 24-Hour Rule

After you study, don't look at the material again for 24 hours. Then test yourself. What did you forget? Those are the gaps that need reinforcement.

This is uncomfortable. You'll realize you remember less than you thought. That's good. Better to discover gaps now than during the test.

Free response questions are learnable. They just require a different kind of practice. Train recall, not recognition. Your test scores will thank you.
